name: Elena Vasquez
background: Principal Backend Engineer at a financial trading platform handling 50K+ concurrent connections. Previously led infrastructure at a real-time collaboration startup. Has personally debugged WebSocket memory leaks in production at 3AM and rebuilt SSE fallback systems under SLA pressure.
expertise: High-throughput persistent connections, connection lifecycle management, load balancer configuration, browser compatibility matrices across 8+ years of production real-time systems.
analytical_approach: Starts from traffic patterns and client behavior: what fraction of connections survive idle, what reconnect storms look like at N+1 scale, and how each protocol behaves under proxy timeouts and CDN edge cases. Works outward from the failure modes.
bias: Leans SSE for unidirectional server-to-client notification workloads. Believes WebSocket is systemically over-applied — most applications pay bidirectional complexity costs for a unidirectional use case. Has empirical scars from debugging opaque WebSocket connection drops where SSE's HTTP error visibility would have saved hours.

name: James Okonkwo
expertise: Client-side networking, progressive enhancement, mobile connectivity, and real-world browser behavior across emerging markets (2G/3G transitions, aggressive proxies, captive portals). Has shipped notification systems to 50M+ MAU across web and mobile-web.
analytical_approach: Evaluates protocols from the client's experience: connection establishment time, automatic reconnection behavior, battery impact, and how each degrades under adverse network conditions. Prioritizes user-perceived reliability over server-side elegance.
background: Staff Frontend Engineer at a large social media platform, formerly Senior SDK Engineer at a push notification SaaS. Witnessed WebSocket implementations fail catastrophically on networks that interrupt long-lived TCP connections (mobile carriers, public WiFi, corporate VPNs).
bias: Prefers WebSocket for any application where the client needs to send data back or where sub-100ms delivery matters. Argues that SSE's HTTP/1.1 connection limits (6 per origin) and lack of built-in binary support create hard ceilings that developers discover only after shipping. Believes the WebSocket reconnection story, while imperfect, is better documented and tested than SSE's spotty EventSource implementation across browsers.

name: Priya Mehta
expertise: Proxy infrastructure, load balancer configuration, protocol inspection, TLS termination, HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 semantics. Has audited real-time systems for enterprises behind Blue Coat, Zscaler, and corporate MITM proxies.
analytical_approach: Traces the full path from server to client, examining what each intermediary does to the connection. Distinguishes between what the specification says and what actually happens when Sophos, F5, or nginx with misconfigured timeouts handles the traffic.
background: Infrastructure Engineer specializing in edge proxy and API gateway architecture at a CDN provider. Previously built WebSocket termination layer for a major cloud provider's load balancer service. Has sysadmin-level knowledge of HTTP proxy behavior that most application developers never encounter.
bias: Argues that protocol choice is secondary to understanding your deployment's proxy topology. Has seen SSE fail behind proxies that don't buffer chunked responses correctly, and WebSocket fail behind proxies that don't handle the Upgrade handshake. Pushes for HTTP/2+SSE as the most proxy-friendly combination, but acknowledges this forces SSE to coexist with HTTP/2's multiplexing semantics. Calibrated skepticism toward any claim that a protocol 'just works' without testing against the actual corporate or CDN infrastructure it will traverse.

name: Marcus Chen
expertise: Backend systems architecture, polyglot service design, and migration patterns. Has rewritten real-time notification subsystems three times at two different companies as requirements evolved from 'show a badge count' to 'sync collaborative document state.'
analytical_approach: Reverses the question: instead of 'which protocol is better,' asks 'what is the minimum viable real-time contract?' Draws a decision tree based on traffic directionality, message ordering guarantees, delivery semantics (at-most-once vs at-least-once), and total system complexity including reconnection, authentication, and monitoring.
background: Distinguished Engineer with 15 years building web infrastructure. Former tech lead for notifications infrastructure at a major cloud collaboration suite. Has publicly argued that most teams spend 80% of their real-time engineering budget on the wrong 20% of the problem (choosing WebSocket when SSE would suffice, or vice versa).
bias: Strongly believes the answer is a polyglot strategy: SSE for typed notification channels (status updates, alerts, badge counts) and WebSocket only when bidirectional or sub-100ms guarantees are actually measured requirements, not assumed ones. Considers 'what if we need bidirectional later' the most dangerous architectural justification — it usually leads to WebSocket complexity for a need that may never materialize. Proposes starting with SSE, measuring, and upgrading specific channels to WebSocket only when the data proves it necessary. Confidence is lower than the others because he's seen both approaches succeed and fail in equal measure; the deciding factor is almost always team expertise, not protocol capability.
